subject.title: Geography
subject.description:
**IB Geography** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **physical and human geography, environmental interactions, global challenges, and spatial patterns**, with a strong focus on **fieldwork, data analysis, case studies, and Geographic Information Systems (GIS)**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Geographic themes - structured questions | 2h 15min | 2h 15min | SL: 35% / HL: 35% |
| **Paper 2** | Geographic perspectives - data response and essays | 1h 15min | 1h 15min | SL: 40% / HL: 45% |
| **Paper 3** | Global interactions - extended essay responses | N/A | 1h | HL: 20% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Fieldwork investigation (2,500 words) | 20 hours | 20 hours | SL: 25% / HL: 20% |

strengths: Clear, concise geographical question with explicit links to the Bradshaw Model and two testable hypotheses
Precise study area description (TenjyÅgawa, Suma Ward, Kobe) with 12 sampling sites and feasibility considerations
Well-labelled locational map with scale bar, north arrow and colour key
weaknesses: Fieldwork question could specify which âfluvial characteristicsâ are under investigation for greater precision
Map would benefit from grid coordinates or GPS references to aid site revisits
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands meet the highest-band descriptors: a clearly framed geographical question that supports hypothesis formation; a precisely identified location allowing primary data collection; and a map that follows mapping conventions. Minor refinements (e.g. specifying variables, adding coordinates) do not detract from top-band performance.
feedback.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

strengths: Methods (float and tape-measure) are described with equations, materials lists and linked to the research question
Implementation includes 12 sites and three trials per site, yielding correctly gathered primary data
weaknesses: Sampling strategy lacks explicit rationale (site spacing, timing, risk assessment)
No critical comparison of alternative methods or deeper discussion of uncertainties in width measurements
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Methods are clearly described and relevant, and data collection is executed correctly, matching the mid-band (2) descriptor. However, the absence of a site-selection rationale, risk assessment and discussion of measurement uncertainties prevents placement in the highest band.

strengths: All raw data directly relate to velocity and channel width downstream trends
Appropriate use of tables and scatter plots with trend lines
weaknesses: Only two variables collectedâlimiting full testing of the Bradshaw Model
Inconsistent unit notation and occasional formatting errors in tables and figures
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Although data are relevant and appropriately presented, the dataset is not fully sufficient (missing discharge, depth, load variables) and there are recurring presentation/convention errors. This aligns with the lower half of the mid-band (score 2) rather than the higher mid-band.
feedback.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

strengths: Use of SRCC and Pearsonâs r with step-by-step calculations and outlier handling
Clear linkage of trends and anomalies to the Bradshaw Model and research hypotheses
weaknesses: No confidence levels or significance testing applied
Analysis omits discussion of measurement error impacts and lacks secondary data integration
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student goes beyond simple description by applying appropriate statistical techniques and linking patterns to theoryâmeriting mid-band performance. However, the absence of significance testing and incomplete gap coverage limits the score to 3 rather than higher.

strengths: Conclusion succinctly ties findings back to hypotheses and the Bradshaw Model
Clear support of conclusions with statistical evidence
weaknesses: Does not acknowledge methodological limitations or contextualise inference scope
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion is clear and fully supported by the analysis (top-band descriptor). A brief mention of limitations would strengthen contextualisation but is not required for full marks, so it receives a 2.

strengths: Lists relevant strengths and weaknesses of methods and data
Suggests sensible improvements (e.g., digital flow meter, refined site selection)
weaknesses: Lacks depth in explaining how proposed improvements would affect data quality and outcomes
Omits consideration of temporal variability and ethical dimensions
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation outlines appropriate strengths/weaknesses and offers relevant suggestions, matching the mid-band. However, it falls short of a comprehensive, impact-focused evaluation needed for the top band, so it earns a 2.

strandSelections.reasoning: Figure 1.1 displays the full river course, labelled sites, scale bar (200 m), colour key and north indicator.  Although the graphic quality is modest, conventional mapping elements are present and enable spatial understanding.  Therefore the submission fulfils the descriptor for âclear locational maps following mapping conventions.â

The student frames a clear, single-sentence fieldwork question that is explicitly geographical and measurable (fluvial characteristics vs. distance downstream).  A full paragraph links the question to the Bradshaw Model and derives two testable hypotheses, demonstrating sound theoretical grounding.  This aligns with the top-band description that the question is geographical with clear theoretical links allowing hypothesis formation.

The student presents raw tables, processed scatter graphs with trend lines, and SRCC/Pearson statistical tables.  These are appropriate and varied, but more sophisticated displays (e.g., box-and-whisker plots or GIS overlays) are absent and some axis labels are small.  Thus the work sits comfortably in the âappropriate techniques usedâ band (score 3).

Twelve sites and three trials per site show correct implementation of float and tape-measure techniques.  Yet limitations (ping-pong buoyancy, ball snagging) introduce notable uncertainty, and only two variables are gathered, so data quantity is moderate rather than extensive.  This fits the band in which techniques are correctly used but yield only minimally robust data.

The analysis employs both SRCC and Pearsonâs r, includes formulae, step-by-step calculations and removal of outliers, showing appropriate descriptive and statistical techniques.  Confidence levels or significance testing are not attempted, preventing placement in the 5â6 band, but the work clearly exceeds simple description; hence top of the 3â4 band (4).

Trends and anomalies (sites 6, 7, 8, 9) are identified and each is linked to theory (tributary input, concrete channel, human activity).  The discussion references both hypotheses and the Bradshaw Model, demonstrating solid linkage, though not exhaustive across all possible variables.  This justifies a 4 within the 3â4 band.

The geographic context section identifies the precise river (TenjyÅgawa, Suma Ward, Kobe), describes accessibility and human influences, and the map pinpoints 12 individual sampling sites.  The detail provided shows that primary data collection is feasible and well planned; hence it meets the highest-band expectation for a precise location with clear feasibility.

The evaluation lists several strengths and limitations and offers brief commentary on their impact, demonstrating more than superficial listing but lacking systematic depth across data quality, methodology and ethics.  This aligns with the middle band (2).

All primary data tables relate directly to velocity and channel width changes downstream; 12 sites give an adequate spread for trend analysis.  Nonetheless, because discharge, depth or load size were not collected, some aspects of the Bradshaw Model cannot be examined, so relevance is good but not complete, meriting the 3â4 band at the lower end (3).

The conclusion restates major findings, references statistical evidence and theory, and explains outliers, providing clear support from analysis.  It meets the descriptor for a âclear conclusion fully supported by analysisâ (band 2).

The analysis section covers velocity and width thoroughly but omits full discussion of measurement error impacts and does not integrate secondary data, leaving some gaps.  Therefore the completeness criterion fits mid-band (score 3).

Velocity and channel-width methods are described in tabular form with equations, materials and justification, demonstrating relevance to the research question.  However, sampling strategy (site spacing rationale, timing, risk assessment) is only implied, and alternative techniques are not critically compared.  This matches the mid-band where methods are described and relevant but not comprehensive.

Most figures carry titles, units and sources; however, occasional formatting issues (inconsistent table borders, missing units in width table) indicate âoccasional errors.â  Therefore the strand matches the 3â4 band with a score of 3 rather than full marks.

For each stated limitation, the student suggests a relevant improvement (e.g., digital flow meter, site selection).  However, explanations of how these changes would influence results are brief, so the work is consistent with ârelevant suggestions outlinedâ rather than the detailed top band.

annotations.comment: To contextualize results further, briefly acknowledge key methodological limitations in the conclusion to frame the scope of inference.
The student frames a concise, geographical fieldwork question that links explicitly to measurable variables and supports hypothesis formation based on the Bradshaw Model.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Consider specifying which fluvial characteristics are meant by âfluvial characteristicsâ in the question to improve precision and clarity for data collection.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The geographic context paragraph clearly situates the study area in Kobeâs Suma Ward, describes accessibility, and acknowledges humanânatural interactions, demonstrating strong context specificity.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Figure 1.1 provides a clear map with a north arrow, scale bar and colour key, adhering well to mapping conventions.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Adding grid coordinates or GPS references on the map would enhance locational precision and enable straightforward site revisits.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The float-method table describes the technique and equation but omits site spacing rationale, timing of sampling and risk assessment, reducing methodological clarity.
Include a brief justification of site selection (e.g. spacing, river features) and a risk assessment to strengthen the linkage between method and fieldwork question.
The occupiedâchannelâwidth method table provides basic setup and materials but does not evaluate potential measurement uncertainties (e.g. tape sag, bank irregularities).
Discuss sources of error for width measurements (e.g., tape tension, vegetation obstruction) and how these were minimised in the field.
The table of float distances has blank header cells and unclear grouping; adding labels and aligning columns will aid readability.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Presenting raw timeâvelocity trials alongside averages demonstrates transparency and allows verification of calculations.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Axis labels in the rawâdata tables omit units (e.g., time in s, velocity in mÂ·sâ»Â¹); ensure units are included in all table headings.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Channelâwidth table lacks consistent unit notation and includes an empty header cell; uniform formatting with units will improve clarity.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: The velocity vs. distance scatter plot (Fig. 3.1.1) effectively shows the trend line and outliers, making downstream patterns clear.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Increase axis label font size and include units directly on the graph axes to improve readability of Fig. 3.1.1 and 3.1.2.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: The SRCC section introduces ties in ranks but does not explain how ties were handled in calculations, which is critical for accuracy.
Define all symbols in the SRCC data table (e.g. âÎ¸â) so that each notation is unambiguous and self-explanatory.
The Pearson correlation formula is presented separately from its discussion; integrating the equation with explanatory text will improve logical flow.
The table of sums for Pearsonâs r is well-structured, allowing replication of the calculation and demonstrating methodological rigor.
The conclusion succinctly ties findings back to hypotheses and the Bradshaw Model, showing clear support from statistical results.
Evaluation lists operational strengths and weaknesses but omits deeper methodological and ethical considerations, limiting its evaluative depth.
Expand improvement suggestions by describing how each proposed change would influence data quality or analysis outcomes.
The evaluation of limitations focuses on equipment issues but does not address temporal variability or seasonal influences, reducing comprehensiveness.
Propose concrete protocols for reducing human error (e.g., standardized training, inter-observer checks) to strengthen the evaluationâs practical relevance.
